context: We see in Genesis four and five, these two genealogies and what a contrast they are.
point_summary: The stark contrast between Genesis 4's ungodly line of Cain and Genesis 5's godly line of Seth frames an eternal-perspective call to be numbered among those who call upon, walk with, and please God.

context: I am the almighty God, El Shaddai
point_summary: Knowing God's name — as when He revealed Himself to Abraham as El Shaddai in Genesis 17:1 — is what grounds faith-filled prayer, enabling the believer to call upon God with confidence in who He is.

context: Genesis chapter 22, as we read of the greatest test that Abraham ever faced in his life
point_summary: Genesis 22 is cited as the scriptural setting for last week's lesson on Jehovah Jireh, where God's provision for Abraham in his greatest test illustrated how God provides for believers today.

context: in the beginning, God
point_summary: God's self-existence, encoded in the name Jehovah (from the Hebrew *hayah*, "to be"), answers the challenge of who created God: no one, because He simply *is*.

context: Genesis chapter number one this evening, Genesis chapter number one
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Genesis chapter number one
point_summary: Though Elohim was not the first name God revealed to mankind historically, it is the first divine name appearing in the pages of Scripture and the most common name for God in the Old Testament, used over 2,300 times.

context: the very first name that we have in the pages of scripture is found in Genesis chapter number one
point_summary: Though Elohim was not the first name God revealed to mankind historically, it is the first divine name appearing in the pages of Scripture and the most common name for God in the Old Testament, used over 2,300 times.

context: in the beginning, God created the heaven and the earth
point_summary: The name Elohim — meaning "strong one" — appears 32 times across Genesis 1's 31 verses, making it the most common divine name in the Old Testament and the first name of God recorded in Scripture.

context: Genesis chapter number one has 31 verses
point_summary: The name Elohim, appearing 32 times across Genesis 1's 31 verses, means "strong one" and establishes an unmistakable connection between God's identity as creator and his power and authority over all creation.

context: he created everything out of nothing. He spoke it all into existence
point_summary: The name Elohim, appearing 32 times in Genesis 1's 31 verses, carries a direct connection to God as creator—the one who spoke everything into existence out of nothing and whose power and authority are displayed through that act.

context: this fundamental truth of Genesis chapter number one
point_summary: The name Elohim appears 32 times in Genesis 1's 31 verses, establishing a direct connection between this first divine name in Scripture and God's identity as all-powerful Creator who spoke everything into existence from nothing.

context: the opening pages of scripture and they try to eliminate, if you will, the really first 11 chapters of the book of Genesis
point_summary: The name Elohim carries a direct connection to creation, identifying God as the one who spoke everything into existence from nothing—a foundational truth Scripture repeats throughout and that unregenerate man attacks to escape accountability to his Creator.

context: The truth of creation can be found throughout the whole of scripture
point_summary: The name Elohim carries a direct connection to creation, establishing that God spoke all things into existence from nothing—a foundational truth Scripture repeats throughout and that unregenerate man attacks to escape accountability to the Creator.

context: In the beginning, God created the heaven and the earth
point_summary: Genesis 1:1 anchors a creation account that Scripture repeatedly affirms throughout its entirety, standing against attempts to discredit it precisely because acknowledging God as Creator makes humanity accountable to Him.

context: and the earth was without form and void and darkness was upon the face of the deep and the spirit of God moved upon the face of the waters

context: Genesis 1, 3

point_summary: The sovereign power of Elohim alone is displayed in Genesis 1:3, where his spoken word — "Let there be light" — immediately produced light, a creative act no other being could perform.

context: God saw the light that it was good and God divided the light from the darkness

context: And God said, let us make man in our image after our likeness and let them have dominion over the fish of the sea and over the fowl of the air and over the cattle and over all the earth and over every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth.

context: and God blessed them. And God said unto them, be fruitful and multiply and replenish the earth and subdue it and have dominion over the fish of the sea and over the fowl of the air and over every living thing that moveth upon the earth.

context: God said, behold, I have given you every herb bearing seed

context: We read in verse 26, let us make man in our image. Plural.

context: the Genesis 1 record dealing with that inherent in the plurality of the term is the Trinity

context: Genesis chapter number one

context: in the garden, in Genesis chapter number 3, that there's this record that God gives us

context: it was already pictured in Genesis chapter number 3

context: verse 5, he had not respect, and Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell

context: and he was uncovered within his tent. And Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father and told his two brethren without

point_summary: Noah's righteousness and walk with God make his drunken exposure a cautionary illustration of alcohol's power to overtake even the godly without their intent.

context: Noah awoke from his wine and knew what his younger son had done unto him

context: Genesis chapter number six, seven, eight, and into chapter number nine

context: in verse number 25, he said, a curse would be Canaan, a servant of a servant shall I be to his brethren

point_summary: The nearly two-month gap between Genesis 8:13 and 8:14 illustrates that Noah waited on God's direction rather than acting on his own observation that the land appeared dry.

context: verse number 15 of Genesis chapter number 8 and the Bible says, and God spake unto Noah, saying
point_summary: After nearly two months of silence between verses 13 and 14, God finally speaks in verse 15, illustrating that divine direction comes in God's timing, not man's, and is given specifically when He is ready.

context: verse number 16. He speaks about Noah, his wife, his sons, and their wives
point_summary: God's command in Genesis 8:16 — directing Noah, his wife, his sons, and their wives off the ark — arrives only after a two-month silence, illustrating that divine direction comes specifically, but in God's timing, not man's.

context: verse number 17. Don't leave the animals on the ark
point_summary: God's specific direction to Noah extended beyond his family to include every living creature, commanding their release so they could fulfill the creation mandate to be fruitful and multiply.

context: Bring forth, verse 17, with thee every living thing that is with thee, of all flesh, both of fowl and of cattle and of every creeping thing that creepeth upon the earth, that they may breed abundantly in the earth and be fruitful and multiply upon the earth
point_summary: God's command in Genesis 8:17 extends beyond Noah's family to encompass every living creature, directing that all flesh brought onto the ark must now disembark to breed, multiply, and repopulate the earth.

context: in verse number 13, Noah opened that door. He opened the covering, and he looks upon the face of the earth
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: as we studied last week in chapter number 7, that God, I believe, led those animals and got them onto the ark
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we read in verses 18 and 19 the obedience to that direction. Noah obeys the Lord
point_summary: God's specific, spoken direction to Noah—naming his family and the animals—is presented as the divine word that finally ends the ark's confinement and calls Noah into renewed obedience.

context: and Noah went forth
point_summary: Noah's exit from the ark in verse 18 is presented as a concrete act of obedience to God's direct command, exemplifying the pattern of faithful compliance that has characterized his life throughout chapters 6–8.

context: every beast, every creeping thing, and every fowl, and whatsoever creepeth upon the earth, after their kinds went forth out of the ark
point_summary: Noah's obedience to God's command to exit the ark is presented as consistent with the pattern of faith and obedience that has characterized his life throughout chapters 6–8.

context: this Bible truth that is given to us in chapter six through eight with regard to the global flood
point_summary: The detailed timeline and dimensions recorded in Genesis 6–8 are presented as evidence against dismissing the global flood as myth, with Jesus and Peter's references to Noah confirming its historical reality.

context: the global flood of Genesis as nothing more than a myth
point_summary: The precise chronological and geographical detail God provides in Genesis 6–8 authenticates the global flood as historical fact, not myth, a reality confirmed by Jesus himself in his earthly teaching.

context: in chapter number eight of Genesis, God remembers Noah, and God causes the wind to come, and causes the waters to be abated
point_summary: The global flood of Genesis 8 is defended as literal history—not myth—on the grounds of its precise timeline, detailed narrative, and corroboration by Jesus and Peter's references to worldwide judgment.

context: at the end of chapter number five, we're given the generations of Noah
point_summary: Genesis 5's genealogical introduction to Noah—listing his generations, age, and children—serves as the threshold through which the reader enters the fuller portrait of a man who walked with and waited on God.

context: Noah walked with God
point_summary: Noah's habit of waiting on God for direction—seen throughout the flood narrative—is traced to his foundational character as one who "walked with God" (Gen. 6:9), a walk that taught him to trust divine timing over personal impulse.

context: Noah didn't get off the ark until God said, Noah, it's time to get off. And I'm thankful for that lesson from Genesis eight.
point_summary: Genesis 8 is presented not merely as historical chronology but as a lesson in waiting for divine direction rather than acting on human reasoning or circumstance.

context: that's what we find that Abram finds out when the king of Salem meets him along the way
point_summary: Melchizedek's identification of God as "the most high God, the possessor of heaven and earth" grounds the sermon's call to trust, illustrating that the Lord who controls all things is the same God in whom David declared his confidence.
